q: Your Databricks job runs OPTIMIZE on a 10TB Delta table nightly, but it's taking 4+ hours and blocking downstream reads. What are your options?
a: OPTIMIZE rewrites files and holds a commit lock during the transaction — on a 10TB unpartitioned table this is a multi-hour window that blocks other writers (readers are protected by snapshot isolation and can still read the previous version). Three options: (1) Shard OPTIMIZE by partition: run OPTIMIZE on one partition at a time in parallel jobs so the lock is narrow and short per-shard; (2) Enable autoOptimize.autoCompact on the table so Databricks spreads small-file compaction continuously across writes instead of doing it all in one nightly job — works best if write frequency is high; (3) Migrate to Liquid Clustering. ALTER TABLE <name> CLUSTER BY (<cols>) — requires DBR 13.3 LTS or above — and then OPTIMIZE runs incrementally, only rewriting files that contain data needing re-clustering. Liquid Clustering's key advantage: no manual ZORDER tuning, no partition scheme to maintain, and OPTIMIZE is safe to run frequently because it only rewrites what needs it. The catch: CLUSTER BY applies to new data immediately; existing files need OPTIMIZE FULL (DBR 16.0+) to be rewritten with the new clustering. For most 10TB+ production tables at Databricks shops, Liquid Clustering is now the recommended path.
fj: I'd increase the cluster size or add more workers to make OPTIMIZE faster.
fs: Throwing compute at a monolithic nightly OPTIMIZE shortens the window but doesn't fix the design. The correct interventions are partitioned OPTIMIZE sharding (narrow locks), auto-compaction (spread the work across writes), or Liquid Clustering (incremental, no manual tune) — not just more workers.
